reasoning: The baseline reference stands at roughly 1.5 (implying a 30-day average of just 0.5 protests over the previous year, or about 6 total over 360 days). The resolution criteria requires more than 10 times this baseline (i.e. >15 protests) in a single rolling 30-day window. If the baseline minimum fell slightly, it would still require strictly >10 protests in a single month. Zambia’s strict public order laws and recent history show that protests are generally contained to highly isolated, singular events—even during times of economic distress or public outrage (such as the recent April 2025 Lusaka march concerning child rape). Generating 11 to 16+ separate ACLED "Protest" events in 30 days would demand an unprecedented nationwide mobilization. With nationwide elections distant (2026) and no massive systemic catalyst emerging in news reports, the likelihood of clearing this exceptionally high bar is extremely low. Uncertainty strictly increases with the forecast horizon, marginally raising the probability for later dates as unforeseen shocks have a slightly larger window to germinate.
